findings.title: Identical symbols on both sides of a mathematical comparison
findings.snippet: then in $M N$-space (where they can be compared directly) $\Lambda_{N}^{M}$ has a greater or lesser density of points per unit volume than does $\Lambda_{N}^{M}$ according to whether $\gamma\left(\Lambda_{N}\right)$ is greater or less than $\gamma\left(\Lambda_{M}\right)$
findings.explanation: The sentence compares 'Λ_N^M' with 'Λ_N^M'—identical on both sides—making the comparison vacuous. The second occurrence should be 'Λ_M^N' (the N-fold Cartesian product of the M-dimensional lattice Λ_M), so that the two MN-dimensional lattices being compared are distinct objects.
findings.fix: Replace the second '$\Lambda_{N}^{M}$' with '$\Lambda_{M}^{N}$', yielding: '…$\Lambda_{N}^{M}$ has a greater or lesser density of points per unit volume than does $\Lambda_{M}^{N}$ according to whether…'

findings.title: Missing word 'if' in 'if and only it'
findings.snippet: A mod-2 complex $\boldsymbol{G}$-lattice $\Lambda$ has depth 1 if and only it the code $C_{1}$ of Lemma 4 is the ( $N, N$ ) code
findings.explanation: 'if and only it' is missing the second 'if'. This is a one-word omission that turns a standard logical phrase ('if and only if') into ungrammatical text, potentially confusing readers about whether 'depth 1' is a sufficient condition, a necessary condition, or both.
findings.fix: Replace 'if and only it' with 'if and only if'.

findings.title: Missing 'Z' in lattice-partition notation 'Z²/4²'
findings.snippet: Note that the $\boldsymbol{Z}^{\mathbf{2}} \boldsymbol{/} \mathbf{4}^{2}$ codes ought to be compared to the one-dimensional $\boldsymbol{Z} / \mathbf{4} \boldsymbol{Z}$ codes, since they have the same redundancy and depth
findings.explanation: The partition is written '4²' (just a scalar) instead of '4Z²'. The sublattice must be '4Z²' (all even-even integer pairs scaled by 4) to be a valid lattice partition of Z². Without the 'Z²' the expression is dimensionally inconsistent with every other partition notation used in the paper.

findings.title: Table XI row: literal string 'Class III codes' appears in the d²_min data column
findings.explanation: The text 'Class III codes' has been inserted into the column that should contain the minimum squared distance d²_min, shifting all subsequent numeric entries one column to the left. The actual d²_min value for the Λ₁₆/RΛ₁₆ 256-state Class II code is 16 (matching the Class II entry for Λ₁₆/RΛ₁₆ listed elsewhere), and γ = 4 (6.02 dB). As printed, the row is unreadable.
findings.fix: Remove 'Class III codes' from the d²_min column and restore the correct numeric value. The corrected row should be: | 16 | $\Lambda_{16}$ | $R\Lambda_{16}$ | 256 | 4/8 | 2 | 16 | 4 | 6.02 | 540 | 2544 | 4.61 |

findings.title: Table XI Class III section: two rows incorrectly merged into one
findings.explanation: The Λ' column contains '$2 D_{4}$ $2 E_{8}$' and the 2^ν column contains '4 16', indicating that two separate data rows have been concatenated into one. The Class III code based on D₄/2D₄ and the Class III code based on E₈/2E₈ should be separate rows. As presented, all parameter values for both codes are uninterpretable.

findings.title: Missing sentence-ending period before 'We call this'
findings.snippet: ascending order We call this the weight distribution of the lattice (theta series, in the lattice literature).
findings.explanation: The sentence 'Because a lattice Λ consists of discrete points, the norms of all lattice points are an infinite set of discrete values that can be enumerated in ascending order' is missing a period at its end, causing it to run together with the next sentence. Without the period the two independent statements are incorrectly fused.
findings.fix: Insert a period after 'ascending order': '…enumerated in ascending order. We call this the weight distribution of the lattice…'

findings.title: Stray closing parenthesis in description of ℝᴺ
findings.snippet: note that $\boldsymbol{R}^{N}$ is itself a group under ordinary vector addition (but not a lattice), because its points are not discrete).
findings.explanation: There is one opening parenthesis for the aside '(but not a lattice)' and two closing parentheses (one after 'lattice' and one at the end of the clause). The trailing ')' is spurious.
findings.fix: Delete the final ')': '…(but not a lattice), because its points are not discrete.'

findings.title: Misspelling: 'tesselation' should be 'tessellation'
findings.snippet: Geometrically, this is a tesselation of $N$-space by translates of fundamental regions of $\Lambda$.
findings.explanation: The standard English spelling requires a double 's': 'tessellation'. The single-'s' form 'tesselation' is a common misspelling.

findings.title: Stray comma inside formula 'R D₄ =, 2Z⁴'
findings.snippet: its dual under this alternative definition is $R D_{4}$, which has code formula $R D_{4} =$, $2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{4}+(4,1,4)$
findings.explanation: A comma appears immediately after the equals sign inside the displayed formula '$R D_{4} =$, $2\boldsymbol{Z}^{4}+(4,1,4)$', breaking the equation and making it syntactically invalid as a mathematical expression.
findings.fix: Remove the comma after '=': '…which has code formula $R D_{4} = 2\boldsymbol{Z}^{4}+(4,1,4)$'

findings.title: Table III: approximation symbol rendered as minus sign ('–2' instead of '~2')
findings.explanation: Every other approximate value in the final column of Table III is formatted as '~2', '~2', etc. The entry '-2' appears to be a rendering artifact where the tilde '~' was converted to a minus sign '−', yielding a negative complexity value that is nonsensical.
findings.fix: Replace '-2' with '~2' in that table cell.

findings.title: Spurious mid-word hyphen: 'Calder-bank-Sloane'
findings.snippet: with the error coefficient for the Calder-bank-Sloane (CS) version (as given in [13]).
findings.explanation: 'Calderbank' is a single surname; the hyphen 'Calder-bank' is a typographic artifact from a line-break in the original typeset paper and should not appear in the transcribed document.

findings.title: Undefined notation 'Λ(n)' used where 'Λ(0,n)' is expected
findings.explanation: Throughout the paper lattices are indexed with two parameters as 'Λ(r, n)'. The notation 'Λ(n)' (one parameter) is never defined. Because Λ(0, n) is stated to be self-dual, the intended expression is almost certainly 'Λ(0, n)'. Using 'Λ(n)' creates ambiguity about whether a different object is intended.

findings.title: Missing hyphen in compound adjective 'onedimensional'
findings.snippet: one fundamental region of the onedimensional integer lattice $\boldsymbol{Z}$ is the half-open interval
findings.explanation: 'onedimensional' should be hyphenated as 'one-dimensional' to match standard usage and the hyphenated form used consistently throughout the rest of the paper (e.g., 'two-dimensional', 'N-dimensional').

findings.title: Inconsistent subscript notation for binomial coefficient: 'C_{nj}' vs 'C_{n_j}'
findings.snippet: $K(r, n)=\sum_{0 \leq j \leq r} C_{n j}$ information bits, where $C_{n_{j}}$ is the combinatorial coefficient $(n!) /[(j!)((n-j)!)]$
findings.explanation: The same binomial coefficient symbol is first written as '$C_{nj}$' (two-character flat subscript) and immediately redefined in the same sentence as '$C_{n_j}$' (nested subscript with underscore). This inconsistency makes the two references look like distinct symbols and may cause typesetting ambiguity.
findings.fix: Pick one form and use it consistently throughout. The most readable is the flat subscript: replace '$C_{n_{j}}$' with '$C_{nj}$'.

findings.title: Spurious mid-word hyphens from line-break artifacts: 'fi-nite-dimensional' and 'par-tial-unit-memory'
findings.snippet: infinite-dimensional generalizations of fi-nite-dimensional sphere-packings … using unit-memory and par-tial-unit-memory convolutional codes
findings.explanation: Both 'fi-nite-dimensional' (line ~1094) and 'par-tial-unit-memory' (line ~1118 in the References section) contain spurious hyphens that are OCR or PDF-to-Markdown conversion artifacts from the original typeset line breaks. They should be 'finite-dimensional' and 'partial-unit-memory' respectively.
